ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw chicken heart as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for raw organ meats is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw chicken heart is approximately 85g (about 3–4 whole hearts), commonly used as a single portion for grilling, stir-frying, or skewering.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, heart, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID: 172853)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 172853 is an exact match for raw chicken heart and is used as the primary nutrient reference. HPB Singapore values for energy (153 kcal), protein (15.55g), fat (9.33g), carbohydrate (0.71g), sodium (74mg), dietary fibre (0.0g), potassium (176mg), calcium (12mg), iron (5.96mg), and cholesterol (136mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data and are retained per the canonical HPB provenance in the input record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, sugar, and additional micronutrients are sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 172853.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — chicken heart, raw contains negligible carbohydrates (0.71g/100g); no GI value assigned per standard practice for high-protein organ meats
gi_source.source_match_notes: Chicken heart is a high-protein, high-fat organ meat with negligible carbohydrate content (0.71g/100g). The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to foods with negligible carbohydrates. Consistent with established GI methodology, glycemic index is not applicable for this food category. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable' per Project Veritas rules for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate per serving is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (74mg/100g) with favourable potassium ratio (176mg/100g); saturated fat approximately 2.67g/100g is below the 5g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Chicken heart in its raw, unseasoned form is low in sodium and may be included in a hypertension-friendly diet, provided it is prepared without high-sodium marinades, sauces, or added salt; the elevated cholesterol content (136mg/100g) warrants moderation for individuals with concurrent dyslipidaemia.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.71g/100g) and high protein (15.55g/100g) result in minimal glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw chicken heart has negligible carbohydrate content and will not raise blood glucose; its high protein content supports satiety and glycaemic stability, making it suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when prepared without high-sugar marinades or glazes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol (136mg/100g) and moderate total fat (9.33g/100g) including saturated fat (~2.67g/100g); no omega-3 fatty acids or anti-inflammatory components present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume chicken heart in moderation due to its elevated dietary cholesterol (136mg/100g) and moderate saturated fat content; frequency should be limited and preparation should avoid added saturated fats or high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No meaningful anti-inflammatory components present; no omega-3, polyphenols, fibre, or antioxidant vitamins in significant quantity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken heart is not recommended as a primary protein source for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the absence of anti-inflammatory factors; if consumed, it should be paired with anti-inflammatory foods such as leafy greens, olive oil, or turmeric-based preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken heart, raw, is an organ meat derived from the heart of the domestic chicken (Gallus gallus domesticus). It is characterised by a deep red to dark maroon colour, a dense and firm muscular texture, and a rich, slightly gamey flavour that becomes more pronounced when cooked. Raw chicken hearts are small, roughly walnut-sized, and are typically sold whole or halved, with visible connective tissue and occasional fat deposits. They are used across a wide range of culinary traditions — grilled on skewers (yakitori in Japan, espetinho de coração in Brazil), stir-fried, braised, or incorporated into offal-based dishes. Nutritionally, chicken heart is a high-protein (15.55g/100g), moderate-fat (9.33g/100g) food with negligible carbohydrate (0.71g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. It is an exceptional source of vitamin B12 (7.28mcg/100g), iron (5.96mg/100g), zinc (4.31mg/100g), selenium (35.5mcg/100g), and riboflavin (0.93mg/100g), supporting red blood cell production, immune function, and energy metabolism. Sodium is low at 74mg/100g in its raw, unseasoned state. Dietary cholesterol is elevated at 136mg/100g, which warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia. Dietary fibre is absent (0g/100g). Chicken heart is halal when sourced from appropriately slaughtered poultry and is gluten-free and dairy-free.

Is chicken heart good for diabetics?
Is chicken heart high in cholesterol?
What vitamins are in chicken heart?
Is chicken heart high in iron?
How do you cook chicken heart?
Is chicken heart good for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore (provenance: canonical, confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172853 for raw chicken heart — an exact match. Saturated fat, micronutrients, and additional nutrients supplemented from USDA SR Legacy exact match. No estimation or analogy required for core macros.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0g/100g: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — chicken heart contains no plant-based fibre. Sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; organ meat contains no free sugars. Trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy for raw chicken heart. vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: detailed vitamin D data for raw chicken heart not available in HPB Singapore database; USDA SR Legacy does not report a reliable vitamin D value for this item. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.71g/100g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Project Veritas rules for low-carbohydrate foods; University of Sydney GI Database consulted and confirms no GI value is assigned for high-protein organ meats with negligible carbohydrate. Monounsaturated fat (2.29g) and polyunsaturated fat (2.29g) values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172853. Cholesterol 136mg/100g warrants moderation for cardiovascular and dyslipidaemia contexts. This is a generic raw ingredient record; cooking method will alter final nutrient profile.
